learning_objectives:
Understand the purpose of Design Patterns in software
architecture
Implement the Singleton pattern for resource management

Apply
the Factory Method pattern to decouple object creation

Use the Observer
pattern for event-driven systems

Implement the Strategy pattern to switch
algorithms at runtime

introduction:
Design patterns are proven solutions to common problems in software design.
Rather than being a specific piece of code, a pattern is a template or a
blueprint for how to solve a problem that occurs in many different situations.
In C++, patterns help us write code that is easier to maintain, extend, and
reuse. We focus on four foundational patterns: Singleton (Creational), Factory
(Creational), Observer (Behavioral), and Strategy (Behavioral).

singleton.definition:
The Singleton pattern
ensures that a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access
to it.
singleton.why_needed:
Useful for managing shared resources like a Database
connection, a Log file, or Configuration settings where having multiple
instances would cause data inconsistency.
singleton.how_it_works:
The constructor is
made private. A static method is used to control the creation of the single
instance, which is stored in a static variable.
singleton.memory_behavior:
The static
instance typically lives in the Data Segment (static memory) or on the Heap if
created via a pointer, persisting for the duration of the program.

factory.definition:
The Factory Method provides an interface for
creating objects but allows subclasses to decide which class to instantiate.
factory.why_needed:
It decouples the client code from the specific classes it needs
to create, making the system easier to extend with new types without modifying
existing code.
factory.how_it_works:
A 'Creator' class declares a method that
returns an object of a 'Product' interface. Subclasses override this method to
return specific 'Concrete Product' instances.

observer.definition:
Defines a one-to-many dependency between objects so that when one object
changes state, all its dependents are notified.
observer.why_needed:
Essential for
creating distributed event-handling systems, like UI buttons or stock market
tickers.
observer.how_it_works:
The 'Subject' keeps a list of 'Observers'. When a
change occurs, the Subject loops through the list and calls a notification
method on each Observer.

strategy.definition:
The Strategy pattern
defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them
interchangeable at runtime.
strategy.why_needed:
Avoids massive switch-cases or
if-else blocks when you have multiple ways to perform a task (e.g., different
sorting algorithms or payment methods).
strategy.how_it_works:
The 'Context' class
contains a pointer to a 'Strategy' interface. The client can swap out the
specific Strategy implementation at any time.

breakdown.explanation:
Prevents other classes from creating
instances using the 'new' keyword.

Holds the single instance of the class across the entire
program.

The 'Gatekeeper'
method that ensures only one instance is ever created.

rules_and_key_points: Design patterns are templates, not libraries.
Singleton: Use private constructors and deleted copy/assignment operators.

Factory: Promotes the 'Open/Closed' principle (open for extension, closed for
modification).

Observer: Relies on an interface so the Subject doesn't need
to know the concrete types of Observers.

Strategy: Use composition (having a
strategy) instead of inheritance.

Always use virtual destructors in base
classes of Factory/Strategy patterns to avoid memory leaks.

C++17 'static
local' variables provide a thread-safe way to implement Singletons (Meyers
Singleton).

Prefer smart pointers (std::unique_ptr) in Factory methods to
manage ownership automatically.

Patterns should only be used when they solve
a specific problem; don't over-engineer.

Interfaces in C++ are implemented
using classes with pure virtual functions.

code_example_1.title: Meyers Singleton (Modern C++17)
code_example_1.description:
The simplest and most robust
way to implement a Singleton in modern C++.
code_example_1.code:
Logger Initialized.
[LOG]: Application started.
[LOG]: Doing some work...

code_example_2.title: Strategy Pattern for Payment Systems
code_example_2.description:
Using the Strategy pattern to switch between Credit Card and
PayPal payments.

code.memory_visualization:
In the Singleton pattern, the 'static Logger instance'
is stored in the static/global memory area. In the Strategy pattern, the
'std::unique_ptr' stores a pointer on the stack that points to an object
(CreditCard or PayPal) residing on the Heap. When 'setPaymentMethod' is called,
the old strategy on the heap is automatically deleted by the unique_ptr.
real_world_use_cases:
Singleton: A Print Spooler managing a single printer
queue.

Factory: A Cross-Platform UI library creating 'WindowsButton' or
'MacButton' based on the OS.

Observer: A News Agency app pushing updates to
thousands of mobile users.

Strategy: A file compression app allowing users to
choose between ZIP, RAR, or 7z algorithms.
common_mistakes:
Singleton:
Forgetting to delete the copy constructor, allowing users to clone the
instance.

Factory: Forgetting 'virtual' destructors, leading to memory leaks
when deleting derived objects.

Observer: Creating circular dependencies where
Subject and Observer depend too heavily on each other's details.

Strategy:
Using the pattern for simple logic that could be a simple function, leading to
'class explosion'.
best_practices:
Prefer the 'Meyers Singleton'
(static local variable) for simplicity and thread safety.

Always use
'std::unique_ptr' or 'std::shared_ptr' when returning objects from a Factory.
Keep interfaces small and focused (Interface Segregation Principle).

Use the
'override' keyword in C++ to ensure you are correctly implementing design
pattern interfaces.

faqs.question: Is Singleton a bad pattern?
faqs.answer:
It is often called an 'anti-pattern' if overused because it
introduces global state, but it is perfectly valid for resource managers like
Loggers or DB connections.

faqs.question:
Why use Factory instead of just
'new'?
faqs.answer:
If you use 'new', your code is hard-coded to a specific
class. Factory lets you change the class without changing the code that uses
it.

key_takeaways:
Creational patterns (Singleton, Factory) handle
object creation.

Behavioral patterns (Observer, Strategy) handle object
communication.
Patterns increase flexibility and decouple code components.
summary.preview_next_topic:
In Phase 15, we will apply these patterns and
everything we've learned so far to build real-world Mini Projects!

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a Singleton and a global variable?
questions.answer:
A global variable does not prevent multiple instances from being
created, whereas a Singleton explicitly controls and restricts instantiation to
exactly one object.

questions.question:
In the Factory Pattern, why is it crucial to have a
virtual destructor in the Base class?
questions.answer:
If the base class destructor
is not virtual, deleting a derived object through a base pointer results in
undefined behavior, usually failing to call the derived destructor and causing
memory leaks.

questions.question:
Implement a 'Configuration' Singleton class that stores a single
string 'appName'.

Create a
Factory Method called 'ShapeFactory' that returns a unique_ptr to either a
'Circle' or 'Square' based on a string input.

questions.answer:
The getInstance() method must
be 'static'. Otherwise, you need an instance to call getInstance(), which
defeats the purpose of a Singleton.

questions.question:
Refactor a code that uses 'if (mode == FAST)
fastSort(); else slowSort();' into a Strategy pattern.
questions.answer:
Define a
SortStrategy interface, implement FastSort and SlowSort as concrete strategies,
and have a Context class hold a pointer to the current strategy.

questions.question:
If an Observer pattern
implementation has 3 observers attached to a Subject, and 'notify()' is called
twice, how many total updates are executed?

Implement a simple Observer pattern where a
'WeatherStation' (Subject) notifies 'PhoneDisplay' (Observer) of temperature
changes.
questions.answer:
Requires an Observer interface with an update(float temp)
method, and a WeatherStation class with a vector of Observer pointers and a
notify() method that iterates through them.

mcqs.question:
Which design pattern is used to provide a single point of access to
a resource?

The
'Open/Closed Principle' is a primary motivation for which pattern?

Given a Strategy pattern where StrategyA prints 'A' and StrategyB prints 'B'.
If we set strategy to A, then switch to B, then call execute(), what is
printed?

In a Factory returning
unique_ptr, what happens to the object when the pointer goes out of scope?

Why would 'Singleton s = Singleton::getInstance();'
fail if the pattern is correctly implemented?

What is missing in this Observer base class: 'class
Observer { public: virtual void update() = 0; };'?

To prevent cloning a Singleton, we use: Singleton(const Singleton&)
= ______;

In the Observer
pattern, the Subject usually stores Observers in a ________.

You are writing a game
where characters can change their combat style (Melee, Ranged, Magic) during a
fight. Which pattern is best?
